case_name: United India Insurance Co. Ltd., Thr ... vs Sukumarbai Suryakant Nikam And Ors on 5 August, 2019
court: High Court of Judicature at Bombay, Bench at Aurangabad

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Suryakant Pandurang Nikam (deceased); claimants: Sukumarbai w/o Suryakant Nikam and sons
compensation_breakdown.interest: together with interest (rate not specified in judgment)

facts: On 15.04.2015 (Tribunal record also at one place references 15.04.2012) deceased Suryakant Pandurang Nikam was a passenger in a Tata Sumo (MH 23/E-3762) returning from a marriage when the vehicle, driven by respondent No.1, skidded near Amba Sakhar Karkhana petrol pump, overturned and occupants were seriously injured. Suryakant was hospitalized at multiple hospitals and succumbed to his injuries after about 17 days of treatment. The claimants (wife and two sons) filed M.A.C.P. No.197/2012 claiming Rs.7,50,000/-, and the Tribunal awarded Rs.7,79,800/- together with interest. The insurance company (appellant) admitted the vehicle was insured but pleaded breach of policy (vehicle used for hire) and denied liability.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued the deceased died due to the negligence of the driver and sought compensation; they relied on Tribunal's application of precedents (including Saju P. Paul, Baljit Kaur, Manuara Khatun) to uphold an order of 'pay and recover' against the insurer so claimants need not wait for recovery from the owner. The claimants did not challenge the quantum of compensation in this appeal.
arguments_respondent: The insurance company contended there was a fundamental breach of policy because the private jeep was hired for a marriage and no extra premium was paid for carrying passengers, so the deceased could not be treated as a 'third party' and insurer had no liability under the Act policy. The owner-driver denied negligence (blamed molasses on road) and insurer relied on authorities limiting use of Article 142 to impose pay-and-recover where insurer has no statutory liability.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that "the term 'any person' used in Section 147 of the Motor Vehicle Act mean 'third Party' but not passenger" and reiterated that "passengers in a private vehicle are not to be treated as 'third party'" when the passenger is travelling in breach of the policy (for hire without coverage). The Court also noted that "Article 142 of the Constitution of India does not cover the cases of pay and recover and that if the Insurance Company has no liability to pay at all, then it cannot be compelled by order of the Court in exercise of its jurisdiction under Article 142... to pay the compensation amount and later on recover it from the owner." Applying these principles, since the insurer proved breach under Section 149(2)(A)(ia) (private jeep hired and no passenger premium), "the Tribunal ought not to have passed the order of 'pay and recover'" and it was an error to hold the insurer jointly and severally liable. The Court therefore set aside the Tribunal's order only to the extent of insurer liability and allowed the insurer to recover any paid amount from the owner.
final_order: Appeal partly allowed. The Tribunal's Judgment and Award dated 28.08.2017 is set aside insofar as it holds the Insurance Company jointly and severally liable; the claim as against the Insurance Company is dismissed and the insurer is entitled to recover the compensation from opponent No.1. Any amount deposited by the insurer to be returned; no order as to costs.
summary: This is a High Court appeal by United India Insurance challenging a Tribunal order that directed the insurer to 'pay and recover' compensation of Rs.7,79,800/- to claimants whose relative died in a Tata Sumo accident. The insurer successfully argued there was a breach of policy because the private jeep was hired and passengers were not covered, so the insurer had no liability as the deceased was not a 'third party' under Section 147. The High Court set aside the pay-and-recover direction against the insurer but left the monetary award intact, permitting the insurer to recover from the owner. Practically, claimants retain the award but the insurer is exonerated from direct liability.
embedding_summary: United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Sukumarbai Suryakant Nikam, High Court of Bombay (Aurangabad Bench), First Appeal No.2021 of 2018, pronounced 05-08-2019. Fatal accident in a Tata Sumo (MH23/E-3762) on 15.04.2015 near Amba Sakhar Karkhana petrol pump; deceased Suryakant Pandurang Nikam died after hospitalization. Tribunal awarded Rs.7,79,800/- with interest and directed insurer to pay and recover; insurer appealed contending breach of policy as private jeep was hired (Section 149(2)(A)(ia), Section 147 MV Act). Key legal issues: validity of 'pay and recover' where insurer has no liability; scope of 'third party' under Section 147; limits on using Article 142 to impose pay-and-recover. Court held passengers in a private vehicle hired without coverage are not 'third party' for insurer liability and Article 142 cannot be used to compel payment where insurer has no statutory liability; appeal partly allowed and insurer exonerated to the extent of liability, award Rs.7,79,800/- remains and insurer may recover from owner.
legal_principles: Passengers in a private vehicle hired for reward without passenger coverage are not 'third party' under Section 147 and insurer has no liability under an Act policy for such passengers
Tribunal should not direct 'pay and recover' against an insurer when the insurer has no statutory liability under the policy
Article 142 of the Constitution cannot be used to compel an insurer to pay compensation if the insurer has no liability to pay at all
Where insurer proves fundamental breach (e.g. vehicle used for hire contrary to policy terms), insurer can be exonerated and allowed to recover amounts from the owner
Beneficial nature of Motor Vehicles Act does not override statutory limits of insurer's liability under the policy
sections_cited: Article 142 of the Constitution of India
cases_cited: National Insurance Co. Ltd. vs. Baljit Kaur and others, (2004) 2 SCC 1
Manager, National Insurance Company Ltd. vs. Saju P. Paul and another, (2013) 2 SCC 41
Manuara Khatun and others vs. Rajesh Kumar Singh and others, 2017 (4) SCC 796
New India Assurance Company Ltd. vs. Asha Rani, AIR 2003 SC 607
Oriental Insurance Company Ltd vs. Devireddy Konda Reddy & Others, (2003) 2 SCC 339
National Insurance Co. Ltd vs. Ajit Kumar And Ors, 2003 A.C.C. 277 (SC)
National Insurance Company Ltd. Vs. Parvathneni, 2009 (8) SCC 785
Swaran Singh case (as followed in Swaran Singh and Laxmi Narain Dhut precedents)
Shamanna vs. Divisional Manager the Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd., 2018 SCC OnLine SC 849
United India Insurance Company Ltd. vs. Anubai Gopichand Thakare and others, 2008(1) Mh.L.J. 73
Traders Pvt. Ltd., Ahmedabad and another vs. Sunanda wd/o Krishna Machivale and others, 2008 SCC OnLine Bom 810
United India Insurance Co. Ltd., Shimla vs. Tilak Singh and others, (2006) 4 SCC 404
bm25_text: United India Insurance Co. Ltd., Thr ... vs Sukumarbai Suryakant Nikam And Ors on 5 August, 2019 High Court of Judicature at Bombay, Bench at Aurangabad 2019 On 15.04.2015 (Tribunal record also at one place references 15.04.2012) deceased Suryakant Pandurang Nikam was a passenger in a Tata Sumo (MH 23/E-3762) returning from a marriage when the vehicle, driven by respondent No.1, skidded near Amba Sakhar Karkhana petrol pump, overturned and occupants were seriously injured. Suryakant was hospitalized at multiple hospitals and succumbed to his injuries after about 17 days of treatment. The claimants (wife and two sons) filed M.A.C.P. No.197/2012 claiming Rs.7,50,000/-, and the Tribunal awarded Rs.7,79,800/- together with interest. The insurance company (appellant) admitted the vehicle was insured but pleaded breach of policy (vehicle used for hire) and denied liability. Claimants argued the deceased died due to the negligence of the driver and sought compensation; they relied on Tribunal's application of precedents (including Saju P. Paul, Baljit Kaur, Manuara Khatun) to uphold an order of 'pay and recover' against the insurer so claimants need not wait for recovery from the owner. The claimants did not challenge the quantum of compensation in this appeal. The insurance company contended there was a fundamental breach of policy because the private jeep was hired for a marriage and no extra premium was paid for carrying passengers, so the deceased could not be treated as a 'third party' and insurer had no liability under the Act policy. The owner-driver denied negligence (blamed molasses on road) and insurer relied on authorities limiting use of Article 142 to impose pay-and-recover where insurer has no statutory liability. The Court held that "the term 'any person' used in Section 147 of the Motor Vehicle Act mean 'third Party' but not passenger" and reiterated that "passengers in a private vehicle are not to be treated as 'third party'" when the passenger is travelling in breach of the policy (for hire without coverage). The Court also noted that "Article 142 of the Constitution of India does not cover the cases of pay and recover and that if the Insurance Company has no liability to pay at all, then it cannot be compelled by order of the Court in exercise of its jurisdiction under Article 142... to pay the compensation amount and later on recover it from the owner." Applying these principles, since the insurer proved breach under Section 149(2)(A)(ia) (private jeep hired and no passenger premium), "the Tribunal ought not to have passed the order of 'pay and recover'" and it was an error to hold the insurer jointly and severally liable. The Court therefore set aside the Tribunal's order only to the extent of insurer liability and allowed the insurer to recover any paid amount from the owner. Appeal partly allowed. The Tribunal's Judgment and Award dated 28.08.2017 is set aside insofar as it holds the Insurance Company jointly and severally liable; the claim as against the Insurance Company is dismissed and the insurer is entitled to recover the compensation from opponent No.1. Any amount deposited by the insurer to be returned; no order as to costs. This is a High Court appeal by United India Insurance challenging a Tribunal order that directed the insurer to 'pay and recover' compensation of Rs.7,79,800/- to claimants whose relative died in a Tata Sumo accident. The insurer successfully argued there was a breach of policy because the private jeep was hired and passengers were not covered, so the insurer had no liability as the deceased was not a 'third party' under Section 147. The High Court set aside the pay-and-recover direction against the insurer but left the monetary award intact, permitting the insurer to recover from the owner. Practically, claimants retain the award but the insurer is exonerated from direct liability. United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v.
